title: Constipation

infobox.value: Infrequent or hard to pass [bowel movements](/wiki/Bowel_movements "Bowel movements"), abdominal pain, bloating[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2)[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3)

Slow movement of stool within the colon, [irritable bowel syndrome](/wiki/Irritable_bowel_syndrome "Irritable bowel syndrome"), [celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease"), [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity"), [pelvic floor](/wiki/Pelvic_floor "Pelvic floor") disorders[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[6]](#cite_note-MakhariaCatassi2015-6)

[Hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [gluten-related disorders](/wiki/Gluten-related_disorders "Gluten-related disorders"), [colon cancer](/wiki/Colon_cancer "Colon cancer"), [ovarian cancer](/wiki/Ovarian_cancer "Ovarian cancer"), [diverticulitis](/wiki/Diverticulitis "Diverticulitis"), [inflammatory bowel disease](/wiki/Inflammatory_bowel_disease "Inflammatory bowel disease"), certain medications[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[6]](#cite_note-MakhariaCatassi2015-6)
Drinking enough fluids, eating more [fiber](/wiki/Dietary_fiber "Dietary fiber"), [exercise](/wiki/Exercise "Exercise")[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)

[Laxatives](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") of the [bulk forming agent](/wiki/Bulk_forming_agent "Bulk forming agent"), [osmotic agent](/wiki/Laxative#Hyperosmotic_agents "Laxative"), [stool softener](/wiki/Stool_softener "Stool softener"), or [lubricant type](/wiki/Laxative#Lubricant_agents "Laxative")[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)

is a [bowel](/wiki/Bowel "Bowel") dysfunction that makes [bowel movements](/wiki/Bowel_movement "Bowel movement") infrequent or hard to pass.[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2) The [stool](/wiki/Human_feces "Human feces") is often hard and dry.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Other symptoms may include abdominal pain, bloating, and feeling as if one has not completely passed the bowel movement.[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3) Complications from constipation may include [hemorrhoids](/wiki/Hemorrhoids "Hemorrhoids"), [anal fissure](/wiki/Anal_fissure "Anal fissure") or [fecal impaction](/wiki/Fecal_impaction "Fecal impaction").[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) The normal frequency of bowel movements in adults is between three per day and three per week.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Babies often have three to four bowel movements per day while young children typically have two to three per day.[[8]](#cite_note-Col2015-8)

| [Risk factors](/wiki/Risk_factor "Risk factor") | [Hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [gluten-related disorders](/wiki/Gluten-related_disorders "Gluten-related disorders"), [colon cancer](/wiki/Colon_cancer "Colon cancer"), [ovarian cancer](/wiki/Ovarian_cancer "Ovarian cancer"), [diverticulitis](/wiki/Diverticulitis "Diverticulitis"), [inflammatory bowel disease](/wiki/Inflammatory_bowel_disease "Inflammatory bowel disease"), certain medications[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[6]](#cite_note-MakhariaCatassi2015-6) |
| Treatment | Drinking enough fluids, eating more [fiber](/wiki/Dietary_fiber "Dietary fiber"), [exercise](/wiki/Exercise "Exercise")[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) |
| [Medication](/wiki/Medication "Medication") | [Laxatives](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") of the [bulk forming agent](/wiki/Bulk_forming_agent "Bulk forming agent"), [osmotic agent](/wiki/Laxative#Hyperosmotic_agents "Laxative"), [stool softener](/wiki/Stool_softener "Stool softener"), or [lubricant type](/wiki/Laxative#Lubricant_agents "Laxative")[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) |

**Constipation** is a [bowel](/wiki/Bowel "Bowel") dysfunction that makes [bowel movements](/wiki/Bowel_movement "Bowel movement") infrequent or hard to pass.[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2) The [stool](/wiki/Human_feces "Human feces") is often hard and dry.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Other symptoms may include abdominal pain, bloating, and feeling as if one has not completely passed the bowel movement.[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3) Complications from constipation may include [hemorrhoids](/wiki/Hemorrhoids "Hemorrhoids"), [anal fissure](/wiki/Anal_fissure "Anal fissure") or [fecal impaction](/wiki/Fecal_impaction "Fecal impaction").[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) The normal frequency of bowel movements in adults is between three per day and three per week.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Babies often have three to four bowel movements per day while young children typically have two to three per day.[[8]](#cite_note-Col2015-8)

Constipation has many causes.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Common causes include slow movement of stool within the colon, [irritable bowel syndrome](/wiki/Irritable_bowel_syndrome "Irritable bowel syndrome"), and [pelvic floor](/wiki/Pelvic_floor "Pelvic floor") disorders.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Underlying associated diseases include [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease"), [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity"), [vitamin B12 deficiency](/wiki/Vitamin_B12_deficiency "Vitamin B12 deficiency"), [colon cancer](/wiki/Colon_cancer "Colon cancer"), [diverticulitis](/wiki/Diverticulitis "Diverticulitis"), and [inflammatory bowel disease](/wiki/Inflammatory_bowel_disease "Inflammatory bowel disease").[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[6]](#cite_note-MakhariaCatassi2015-6)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Medications associated with constipation include [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids"), certain [antacids](/wiki/Antacids "Antacids"), [calcium channel blockers](/wiki/Calcium_channel_blockers "Calcium channel blockers"), and [anticholinergics](/wiki/Anticholinergics "Anticholinergics").[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Of those taking [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") about 90% develop constipation.[[11]](#cite_note-Cad2014-11) Constipation is more concerning when there is weight loss or [anemia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia"), [blood is present in the stool](/wiki/Hematochezia "Hematochezia"), there is a history of inflammatory bowel disease or colon cancer in a person's family, or it is of new onset in someone who is older.[[12]](#cite_note-Br2016-12)

Treatment of constipation depends on the underlying cause and the duration that it has been present.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Measures that may help include drinking enough fluids, eating more [fiber](/wiki/Dietary_fiber "Dietary fiber"), and [exercise](/wiki/Exercise "Exercise").[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) If this is not effective, [laxatives](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") of the [bulk-forming agent](/wiki/Bulk-forming_agent "Bulk-forming agent"), [osmotic agent](/wiki/Laxative#Hyperosmotic_agents "Laxative"), [stool softener](/wiki/Stool_softener "Stool softener"), or [lubricant type](/wiki/Laxative#Lubricant_agents "Laxative") may be recommended.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) [Stimulant laxatives](/wiki/Stimulant_laxatives "Stimulant laxatives") are generally reserved for when other types are not effective.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4) Other treatments may include [biofeedback](/wiki/Biofeedback "Biofeedback") or in rare cases surgery.[[4]](#cite_note-NIH2015-4)

In the general population rates of constipation are 2–30 percent.[[7]](#cite_note-And2006-7) Among elderly people living in a care home the rate of constipation is 50–75 percent.[[11]](#cite_note-Cad2014-11)

## Definition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/BristolStoolChart.png/500px-BristolStoolChart.png)](/wiki/File:BristolStoolChart.png)

[Bristol stool scale](/wiki/Bristol_stool_scale "Bristol stool scale")

Constipation is a symptom, not a disease. Most commonly, constipation is thought of as infrequent bowel movements, usually fewer than 3 stools per week.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, people may have other complaints as well including:[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3)[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)

* Straining with bowel movements
* Excessive time needed to pass a bowel movement
* Hard stools
* Pain with bowel movements secondary to straining
* Abdominal pain
* Abdominal bloating.
* the sensation of incomplete bowel evacuation.

The [Rome IV Criteria](/wiki/Rome_process "Rome process") are a set of symptoms that help standardize the diagnosis of constipation in various age groups. These criteria help physicians to better define constipation in a standardized manner.

## Causes

The causes of constipation can be divided into [congenital](/wiki/Congenital "Congenital"), primary, and secondary.[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2) The most common kind is primary and not life-threatening.[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16) It can also be divided by the age group affected such as children and adults.

Primary or [functional constipation](/wiki/Functional_constipation "Functional constipation") is defined by ongoing symptoms for greater than six months not due to an underlying cause such as medication [side effects](/wiki/Side_effects "Side effects") or an underlying medical condition.[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2)[[17]](#cite_note-AFP05-17) It is not associated with abdominal pain, thus distinguishing it from [irritable bowel syndrome](/wiki/Irritable_bowel_syndrome "Irritable bowel syndrome").[[2]](#cite_note-Review09-2) It is the most common kind of constipation, and is often multifactorial.[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16)[[18]](#cite_note-CC:CriticalReview-18) In adults, such primary causes include: dietary choices such as insufficient dietary fiber or fluid intake, or behavioral causes such as decreased [physical activity](/wiki/Physical_activity "Physical activity"). In children, causes can include diets low in fiber and fluids, underlying medical conditions, and reluctance to go to the bathroom.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the elderly, common causes have been attributed to insufficient dietary fiber intake, inadequate fluid intake, decreased [physical activity](/wiki/Physical_activity "Physical activity"), side effects of medications, [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), and obstruction by [colorectal cancer](/wiki/Colorectal_cancer "Colorectal cancer").[[20]](#cite_note-Le2007-20) Evidence to support these factors however is poor.[[20]](#cite_note-Le2007-20)

Secondary causes include side effects of medications such as opiates, endocrine and metabolic disorders such as [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), and obstruction such as from [colorectal cancer](/wiki/Colorectal_cancer "Colorectal cancer")[[18]](#cite_note-CC:CriticalReview-18) or [ovarian cancer](/wiki/Ovarian_cancer "Ovarian cancer").[[21]](#cite_note-CDCsep2016-21) [Celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease") and [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity") may also present with constipation.[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[22]](#cite_note-WGO2016-22)[[6]](#cite_note-MakhariaCatassi2015-6) Cystocele can develop as a result of chronic constipation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Diet

Constipation can be caused or exacerbated by a low-fiber diet, low liquid intake, or dieting.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[24]](#cite_note-mccallum-24) Dietary fiber helps to decrease colonic transport time, increases stool bulk but simultaneously softens stool. Therefore, diets low in fiber can lead to primary constipation.[[18]](#cite_note-CC:CriticalReview-18)

### Medications

Many medications have constipation as a side effect. Some include (but are not limited to) [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid"), [diuretics](/wiki/Diuretics "Diuretics"), [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant"), [antihistamines](/wiki/Antihistamine "Antihistamine"), [antispasmodics](/wiki/Antispasmodic "Antispasmodic"), [anticonvulsants](/wiki/Anticonvulsant "Anticonvulsant"), [tricyclic antidepressants](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressant "Tricyclic antidepressant"), [antiarrythmics](/wiki/Antiarrythmics "Antiarrythmics"), [beta-adrenoceptor antagonists](/wiki/Beta_blocker "Beta blocker"), [anti-diarrheals](/wiki/Anti-diarrheal "Anti-diarrheal"), 5-HT3 receptor antagonists such as [ondansetron](/wiki/Ondansetron "Ondansetron"), and aluminum [antacids](/wiki/Antacid "Antacid").[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[25]](#cite_note-ap01-25) Certain [calcium channel blockers](/wiki/Calcium_channel_blocker "Calcium channel blocker") such as [nifedipine](/wiki/Nifedipine "Nifedipine") and [verapamil](/wiki/Verapamil "Verapamil") can cause severe constipation due to dysfunction of motility in the [rectosigmoid colon](/wiki/Rectosigmoid_colon "Rectosigmoid colon").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Supplements such as calcium and iron supplements can also have constipation as a notable side effect.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-q220-28)

### Medical conditions

Metabolic and endocrine problems which may lead to constipation include: [pheochromocytoma](/wiki/Pheochromocytoma "Pheochromocytoma"), [hypercalcemia](/wiki/Hypercalcemia "Hypercalcemia"), [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), [hyperparathyroidism](/wiki/Hyperparathyroidism "Hyperparathyroidism"), [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria"), [chronic kidney disease](/wiki/Chronic_kidney_disease "Chronic kidney disease"), [pan-hypopituitarism](/wiki/Pan-hypopituitarism "Pan-hypopituitarism"), [diabetes mellitus](/wiki/Diabetes_mellitus "Diabetes mellitus"), and [cystic fibrosis](/wiki/Cystic_fibrosis "Cystic fibrosis").[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16) Constipation is also common in individuals with muscular and myotonic dystrophy.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)

[Systemic diseases](/wiki/Systemic_disease "Systemic disease") that may present with constipation include [celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease") and [systemic sclerosis](/wiki/Systemic_sclerosis "Systemic sclerosis").[[5]](#cite_note-SymptomsCausesNIDDK-5)[[22]](#cite_note-WGO2016-22)[[29]](#cite_note-GygerBaron2015-29)

Constipation has a number of structural (mechanical, morphological, anatomical) causes, namely through creating space-occupying lesions within the colon that stop the passage of stool, such as [colorectal cancer](/wiki/Colorectal_cancer "Colorectal cancer"), [strictures](/wiki/Stenosis "Stenosis"), [rectocoles](/wiki/Rectocele "Rectocele"), [anal sphincter](/wiki/Human_anus "Human anus") damage or malformation and post-surgical changes. Extra-intestinal masses such as other malignancies can also lead to constipation from external compression.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Constipation also has neurological causes, including [anismus](/wiki/Anismus "Anismus"), [descending perineum syndrome](/wiki/Descending_perineum_syndrome "Descending perineum syndrome"), [desmosis](/wiki/Desmosis "Desmosis") and [Hirschsprung's disease](/wiki/Hirschsprung%27s_disease "Hirschsprung's disease").[[7]](#cite_note-And2006-7) In infants, Hirschsprung's disease is the most common medical disorder associated with constipation. Anismus occurs in a small minority of persons with chronic constipation or obstructed defecation.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Spinal cord lesions and neurological disorders such as [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") and [pelvic floor dysfunction](/wiki/Pelvic_floor_dysfunction "Pelvic floor dysfunction")[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16) can also lead to constipation.

[Chagas disease](/wiki/Chagas_disease "Chagas disease") may cause constipation through the destruction of the [myenteric plexus](/wiki/Myenteric_plexus "Myenteric plexus").[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Psychological

Voluntary withholding of the stool is a common cause of constipation.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15) The choice to withhold can be due to factors such as fear of pain, fear of public restrooms, or laziness.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15) When a child holds in the stool a combination of encouragement, [fluids](/wiki/Drink "Drink"), [fiber](/wiki/Dietary_fiber "Dietary fiber"), and [laxatives](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") may be useful to overcome the problem.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Early intervention with withholding is important as this can lead to [anal fissures](/wiki/Anal_fissure "Anal fissure").[[35]](#cite_note-AmericanGATReview-35)

### Congenital

[A number of diseases present at birth](/wiki/Congenital_disorder "Congenital disorder") can result in [constipation in children](/wiki/Constipation_in_children "Constipation in children"). They are as a group uncommon with Hirschsprung's disease (HD) being the most common.[[36]](#cite_note-36) There are also congenital structural anomalies that can lead to constipation, including anterior displacement of the anus, [imperforate anus](/wiki/Imperforate_anus "Imperforate anus"), strictures, and small left colon syndrome.[[37]](#cite_note-JournalOPGAN-37)

## Diagnosis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Constipation%28lots%29.png/250px-Constipation%28lots%29.png)](/wiki/File:Constipation(lots).png)

Significant constipation in the plain X-ray of an 8-year-old

The diagnosis is typically made based on a person's description of the symptoms. Bowel movements that are difficult to pass, very firm, or made up of small hard pellets (like those excreted by rabbits) qualify as constipation, even if they occur every day. Constipation is traditionally defined as three or fewer bowel movements per week.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13) Other symptoms related to constipation can include bloating, [distension](/wiki/Abdominal_distension "Abdominal distension"), abdominal pain, headaches, a feeling of fatigue and nervous exhaustion, or a sense of incomplete emptying.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Although constipation may be a diagnosis, it is typically viewed as a symptom that requires evaluation to discern a cause.

### Description

Distinguish between acute (days to weeks) or chronic (months to years) onset of constipation because this information changes the [differential diagnosis](/wiki/Differential_diagnosis "Differential diagnosis"). This in the context of accompanied symptoms helps physicians discover the cause of constipation. People often describe their constipation as bowel movements that are difficult to pass, firm stool with lumpy or hard consistency, and excessive straining during bowel movements. Bloating, abdominal [distension](/wiki/Abdominal_distension "Abdominal distension"), and abdominal pain often accompany constipation.[[39]](#cite_note-LTierneyBook-39) Chronic constipation (symptoms present at least three days per month for more than three months) associated with abdominal discomfort is often diagnosed as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) when no obvious cause is found.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Poor dietary habits, previous abdominal surgeries, and certain medical conditions can contribute to constipation. Diseases associated with constipation include [hypothyroidism](/wiki/Hypothyroidism "Hypothyroidism"), certain types of [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), and [irritable bowel syndrome](/wiki/Irritable_bowel_syndrome "Irritable bowel syndrome"). Low fiber intake, inadequate amounts of fluids, poor ambulation or immobility, or medications can contribute to constipation.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[24]](#cite_note-mccallum-24) Once the presence of constipation is identified based on a culmination of the symptoms described above, then the cause of constipation should be figured out.

Separating non-life-threatening from serious causes may be partly based on symptoms. For example, colon cancer may be suspected if a person has a family history of colon cancer, fever, weight loss, and rectal bleeding.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13) Other alarming signs and symptoms include family or personal history of inflammatory bowel disease, age of onset over 50, change in stool caliber, nausea, vomiting, and neurological symptoms like weakness, numbness and difficulty urinating.[[39]](#cite_note-LTierneyBook-39)

### Examination

A physical examination should involve at least an abdominal exam and rectal exam. Abdominal exam may reveal an abdominal mass if there is significant stool burden and may reveal abdominal discomfort. [Rectal examination](/wiki/Rectal_examination "Rectal examination") gives an impression of the anal sphincter [tone](/wiki/Muscle_tone "Muscle tone") and whether the lower rectum contains any feces or not. Rectal examination also gives information on the consistency of the stool, the presence of hemorrhoids, blood and whether any [perineal](/wiki/Perineum "Perineum") irregularities are present including skin tags, fissures, anal warts.[[24]](#cite_note-mccallum-24)[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13) Physical examination is done manually by a physician and is used to guide which diagnostic tests to order.

### Diagnostic tests

Functional constipation is common and does not warrant diagnostic testing. Imaging and laboratory tests are typically recommended for those with alarm signs or symptoms.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13)

The laboratory tests performed depends on the suspected underlying cause of the constipation. Tests may include CBC ([complete blood count](/wiki/Complete_blood_count "Complete blood count")), thyroid function tests, serum calcium, serum potassium, etc.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13)

[Abdominal X-rays](/wiki/Abdominal_radiography "Abdominal radiography") are generally only performed if bowel obstruction is suspected, may reveal extensive impacted fecal matter in the colon, and may confirm or rule out other causes of similar symptoms.[[24]](#cite_note-mccallum-24)[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)

Colonoscopy may be performed if an abnormality in the colon like a tumor is suspected.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13) Other tests rarely ordered include [anorectal manometry](/wiki/Anorectal_manometry "Anorectal manometry"), anal sphincter electromyography, and [defecography](/wiki/Defecography "Defecography").[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)

Colonic propagating pressure wave sequences (PSs) are responsible for discrete movements of the bowel contents and are vital for normal defecation. Deficiencies in PS frequency, amplitude, and extent of propagation are all implicated in severe defecatory dysfunction (SDD). Mechanisms that can normalize these aberrant motor patterns may help rectify the problem. Recently the novel therapy of [sacral nerve stimulation](/wiki/Sacral_nerve_stimulation "Sacral nerve stimulation") (SNS) has been utilized for the treatment of severe constipation.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Criteria

The Rome III Criteria for functional constipation must include two or more of the following and present for the past three months, with symptoms starting for at least 6 months prior to diagnosis.[[13]](#cite_note-Jam2011-13)

* Straining during defecation for at least 25% of bowel movements
* Lumpy or hard stools in at least 25% of defecations
* Sensation of incomplete evacuation for at least 25% of defecations
* Sensation of anorectal obstruction/blockage for at least 25% of defecations
* Manual maneuvers to facilitate at least 25% of defecations
* Fewer than 3 defecations per week
* Loose stools are rarely present without the use of laxatives
* There are insufficient criteria for irritable bowel syndrome

## Prevention

Constipation is usually easier to prevent than to treat. Following the relief of constipation, maintenance with adequate exercise, fluid intake, and high-fiber diet is recommended.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)

## Treatment

A limited number of cases require urgent medical intervention or will result in severe consequences.[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3)

The treatment of constipation should focus on the underlying cause if known. The National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) break constipation in adults into two categories: chronic constipation of unknown cause, and constipation due to opiates.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In chronic constipation of unknown cause, the main treatment involves the increased intake of water and fiber (either dietary or as supplements).[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16) The routine use of laxatives or enemas is discouraged, as having bowel movements may come to be dependent upon their use.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Fiber supplements

Soluble fiber supplements such as [psyllium](/wiki/Psyllium "Psyllium") are generally considered first-line treatment for chronic constipation, compared to insoluble fibers such as wheat bran. Side effects of fiber supplements include bloating, flatulence, diarrhea, and possible [malabsorption](/wiki/Malabsorption "Malabsorption") of iron, calcium, and some medications. However, patients with opiate-induced constipation will likely not benefit from fiber supplements.[[35]](#cite_note-AmericanGATReview-35)

### Laxatives

If [laxatives](/wiki/Laxatives "Laxatives") are used, [milk of magnesia](/wiki/Milk_of_magnesia "Milk of magnesia") or [polyethylene glycol](/wiki/Polyethylene_glycol "Polyethylene glycol") are recommended as first-line agents due to their low cost and safety.[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3) Stimulants should only be used if this is not effective.[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16) In cases of chronic constipation, polyethylene glycol appears superior to [lactulose](/wiki/Lactulose "Lactulose").[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Prokinetics](/wiki/Prokinetic_agent "Prokinetic agent") may be used to improve gastrointestinal motility. A number of new agents have shown positive outcomes in chronic constipation; these include [prucalopride](/wiki/Prucalopride "Prucalopride")[[45]](#cite_note-45) and [lubiprostone](/wiki/Lubiprostone "Lubiprostone").[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Cisapride](/wiki/Cisapride "Cisapride") is widely available in third world countries, but has been withdrawn in most of the west. It has not been shown to have a benefit on constipation, while potentially causing cardiac arrhythmias and deaths.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Enemas

[Enemas](/wiki/Enema "Enema") can be used to provide a form of mechanical stimulation. A large volume or high enema[[48]](#cite_note-48) can be given to cleanse as much of the colon as possible of feces,[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) and the solution administered commonly contains [castile soap](/wiki/Castile_soap "Castile soap") which irritates the colon's lining resulting in increased urgency to defecate.[[51]](#cite_note-Safety_and_effectiveness-51) However, a low enema is generally useful only for stool in the rectum, not in the intestinal tract.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Physical intervention

Constipation that resists the above measures may require physical intervention such as manual disimpaction (the physical removal of impacted stool using the hands; see [fecal impaction](/wiki/Fecal_impaction "Fecal impaction")).

### Regular exercise

Regular [exercise](/wiki/Exercise "Exercise") can help improve chronic constipation.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Behavioral interventions

In children, constipation is often managed as part of broader behavioral programs aimed at improving bowel habits. These programs may include scheduled toilet sitting, education about bowel physiology, adequate hydration, and measures to normalize stool consistency. In some pediatric clinics, these strategies are delivered within structured “[urotherapy](/wiki/Urotherapy "Urotherapy")” programs, which emphasize regular toileting routines and coordinated behavioral management. Because constipation is a core component of bladder and bowel dysfunction (BBD), addressing bowel habits is considered essential for improving overall symptoms in affected children.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Surgical intervention

In refractory cases, procedures can be performed to help relieve constipation. [Sacral nerve stimulation](/wiki/Sacral_nerve_stimulation "Sacral nerve stimulation") has been demonstrated to be effective in a minority of cases. [Colectomy](/wiki/Colectomy "Colectomy") with ileorectal [anastomosis](/wiki/Anastomosis "Anastomosis") is another intervention performed only in patients known to have a slow colonic transit time and in whom a defecation disorder has either been treated or is not present.[[3]](#cite_note-AGA2013-3) Because this is a major operation, side effects can include considerable abdominal pain, small bowel obstruction, and post-surgical infections. Furthermore, it has a very variable rate of success and is very case dependent.[[35]](#cite_note-AmericanGATReview-35)

## Prognosis

Complications that can arise from constipation include [hemorrhoids](/wiki/Hemorrhoids "Hemorrhoids"), [anal fissures](/wiki/Anal_fissures "Anal fissures"), [rectal prolapse](/wiki/Rectal_prolapse "Rectal prolapse"), and fecal impaction.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)[[24]](#cite_note-mccallum-24)[[59]](#cite_note-bharucha-59)[[60]](#cite_note-niddk-60) Straining to pass stool may lead to hemorrhoids. In later stages of constipation, the abdomen may become distended, hard and diffusely tender. Severe cases ("fecal impaction" or *malignant constipation*) may exhibit symptoms of bowel obstruction (nausea, [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting"), tender abdomen) and [encopresis](/wiki/Encopresis "Encopresis"), where soft stool from the small intestine bypasses the mass of impacted fecal matter in the [colon](/wiki/Colon_(anatomy) "Colon (anatomy)").

## Epidemiology

Constipation is the most common chronic gastrointestinal disorder in adults. Depending on the definition employed, it occurs in 2% to 20% of the population.[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16)[[61]](#cite_note-Epi89-61) It is more common in women, the elderly and children.[[61]](#cite_note-Epi89-61) Specifically constipation with no known cause affects females more often affected than males.[[62]](#cite_note-chang-62) The reasons it occurs more frequently in the elderly is felt to be due to an increasing number of health problems as humans age and decreased physical activity.[[17]](#cite_note-AFP05-17)

* 12% of the population worldwide reports having constipation.[[63]](#cite_note-63)
* Chronic constipation accounts for 3% of all visits annually to pediatric outpatient clinics.[[15]](#cite_note-walia-15)
* Constipation-related health care costs total $6.9 billion in the US annually.[[16]](#cite_note-AGA2000-16)
* More than four million Americans have frequent constipation, accounting for 2.5 million physician visits a year.[[60]](#cite_note-niddk-60)
* Around $725 million is spent on laxative products each year in America.[[60]](#cite_note-niddk-60)

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/A_monkey_rejects_the_old_style_clyster_for_his_new_%27clyso-pompe%27%2C_which_he_fills_with_opium_and_marshmallow_Wellcome_V0011775.jpg/250px-A_monkey_rejects_the_old_style_clyster_for_his_new_%27clyso-pompe%27%2C_which_he_fills_with_opium_and_marshmallow_Wellcome_V0011775.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_monkey_rejects_the_old_style_clyster_for_his_new_%27clyso-pompe%27,_which_he_fills_with_opium_and_marshmallow_Wellcome_V0011775.jpg)

19th century satirical cartoon of a monkey rejecting an old style clyster for a new design, filled with marshmallow and opium

Since ancient times different societies have published medical opinions about how health care providers should respond to constipation in patients.[[64]](#cite_note-inner_hygiene-64) In various times and places, doctors have made claims that constipation has all sorts of medical or social causes.[[64]](#cite_note-inner_hygiene-64) Doctors in history have treated constipation in reasonable and unreasonable ways, including use of a [spatula mundani](/wiki/Spatula_Mundani "Spatula Mundani").[[64]](#cite_note-inner_hygiene-64)

After the advent of the [germ theory of disease](/wiki/Germ_theory_of_disease "Germ theory of disease") then the idea of "auto-intoxication" entered popular Western thought in a fresh way.[[64]](#cite_note-inner_hygiene-64) [Enema](/wiki/Enema "Enema") as a scientific medical treatment and [colon cleansing](/wiki/Colon_cleansing "Colon cleansing") as alternative medical treatment became more common in medical practice.[[64]](#cite_note-inner_hygiene-64)

Since the 1700s in the West there has been some popular thought that people with constipation have some moral failing with [gluttony](/wiki/Gluttony "Gluttony") or [laziness](/wiki/Laziness "Laziness").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

## Special populations

### Children

Main article: [Constipation in children](/wiki/Constipation_in_children "Constipation in children")

Approximately 3% of children have constipation, with girls and boys being equally affected.[[37]](#cite_note-JournalOPGAN-37) With constipation accounting for approximately 5% of general pediatrician visits and 25% of pediatric gastroenterologist visits, the symptom carries a significant financial impact upon the healthcare system.[[8]](#cite_note-Col2015-8) While it is difficult to assess an exact age at which constipation most commonly arises, children frequently experience constipation in conjunction with life-changes. Examples include: toilet training, starting or transferring to a new school, and changes in diet.[[8]](#cite_note-Col2015-8) Especially in infants, changes in formula or transitioning from breast milk to formula can cause constipation. The majority of constipation cases are not tied to a medical disease, and treatment can be focused on simply relieving the symptoms.[[37]](#cite_note-JournalOPGAN-37)

### Postpartum women

The six-week period after pregnancy is called the [postpartum](/wiki/Postpartum "Postpartum") stage.[[66]](#cite_note-CochraneDatabase-66) During this time, women are at increased risk of being constipated. Multiple studies estimate the prevalence of constipation to be around 25% during the first 3 months.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Constipation can cause discomfort for women, as they are still recovering from the delivery process especially if they have had a [perineal tear](/wiki/Perineal_tear "Perineal tear") or underwent an [episiotomy](/wiki/Episiotomy "Episiotomy").[[68]](#cite_note-:0-68) Risk factors that increase the risk of constipation in this population include:[[68]](#cite_note-:0-68)

* Damage to the levator ani muscles ([pelvic floor muscles](/wiki/Pelvic_floor "Pelvic floor")) during childbirth
* Forceps-assisted delivery
* Lengthy second stage of labor
* Delivering a large child
* Hemorrhoids

[Hemorrhoids](/wiki/Hemorrhoid "Hemorrhoid") are common in pregnancy and also may get exacerbated when constipated. Anything that can cause pain with stooling (hemorrhoids, perineal tear, episiotomy) can lead to constipation because patients may withhold from having a bowel movement so as to avoid pain.[[68]](#cite_note-:0-68)

The pelvic floor muscles play an important role in helping pass a bowel movement. Injury to those muscles by some of the above risk factors (examples- delivering a large child, lengthy second stage of labor, forceps delivery) can result in constipation.[[68]](#cite_note-:0-68) Enemas may be administered during labor and these can also alter bowel movements in the days after giving birth.[[66]](#cite_note-CochraneDatabase-66) However, there is insufficient evidence to make conclusions about the effectiveness and safety of laxatives in this group of people.[[68]](#cite_note-:0-68)
